name: UX Testing & Validation
description: Comprehensive guide to user experience testing methodologies, validation techniques, and research methods

usability_testing.description: Observing users attempting tasks to identify usability issues
usability_testing.when: Throughout design process (early = wireframes, later = high-fidelity)

1_prepare.tasks: Create 3-5 realistic tasks users would perform

2_recruit.participants: 5-8 users per user group (Nielsen: 5 users find 85% of issues)

4_analyze.rainbow_spreadsheet: Track issues per participant in color-coded grid

tasks_best_practices: Use realistic scenarios (not 'Find the About page')
Don't use exact UI labels in task description
Start with easy tasks, progress to harder

unmoderated.description: Users complete tasks remotely without facilitator
unmoderated.best_for: Quantitative data, benchmarking, A/B testing validation

guerrilla_testing.description: Quick, informal testing in public places
guerrilla_testing.when: Early concepts, rapid feedback, low budget

a_b_testing.description: Comparing two versions to determine which performs better

1_hypothesis.example: If we change CTA from 'Submit' to 'Get Started', then click-through rate will increase because it's more actionable

2_design.one_change: Change ONE variable at a time (not multiple)
2_design.sample_size: Use A/B test calculator to determine required traffic

4_analyze.primary_metric: The metric you're optimizing (CTR, conversion, revenue)
4_analyze.secondary_metrics: Other impacts (bounce rate, time on page)
4_analyze.segments: Analyze by device, traffic source, user type

high_impact: Pricing display (monthly vs annual, visual hierarchy)

best_practices: Test one variable at a time
Run tests long enough (at least 1 week, ideally 2-4)
Consider external factors (holidays, seasonality, marketing campaigns)
Don't stop test early (even if winning)
common_mistakes: Testing too many variables at once
Ignoring external factors (Black Friday, email campaign)
Testing low-traffic pages (takes too long to reach significance)

multivariate_testing.description: Testing multiple variables simultaneously to find best combination
multivariate_testing.vs_ab: A/B tests one change; MVT tests multiple changes and interactions
multivariate_testing.requirement: Requires significantly more traffic than A/B testing

first_click_testing.description: Test where users click first for a given task
first_click_testing.finding: If first click is correct, 87% chance user completes task successfully

five_second_test.description: Show design for 5 seconds, then ask what they remember
five_second_test.purpose: Test first impressions, visual hierarchy, key message
five_second_test.use_when: Landing pages, homepages, ads, early concepts

card_sorting.description: Users organize content cards into groups
card_sorting.purpose: Understand mental models, inform information architecture

open.description: Users create and name their own groups

hybrid.description: Predefined groups + option to create new

process.1_prepare: Create cards (one item per card, 30-60 cards)

best_practices: Use realistic content (actual page names, not lorem ipsum)
Card labels should be clear and specific
Look for patterns (> 60% agreement is strong)

tree_testing.description: Test findability in a text-based hierarchy (no visual design)
tree_testing.purpose: Validate IA before investing in design

process.2_define_tasks: Find-tasks ('Where would you find your order history?')
process.4_test: Users navigate tree by clicking categories

surveys_questionnaires.description: Collect quantitative and qualitative data at scale
surveys_questionnaires.when: Post-launch, to understand attitudes and satisfaction

sus.name: System Usability Scale
questions: I think I would like to use this system frequently
I found the system unnecessarily complex
I thought the system was easy to use
I think I would need support to use this system
I found the various functions well integrated

nps.name: Net Promoter Score
nps.question: How likely are you to recommend this to a friend? (0-10)
nps.follow_up: Why did you give that score?

umux_lite.name: UMUX-Lite
questions: This system's capabilities meet my requirements
This system is easy to use

single_ease_question.question: Overall, how difficult or easy was the task to complete?
single_ease_question.when: After each task in usability test

question_types.likert_scale: Strongly Disagree to Strongly Agree (1-5 or 1-7)

timing.exit: On page exit or session end

analytics_analysis.description: Analyze user behavior data to identify UX issues

engagement.time_on_page: How long users spend (longer = more engaged, unless stuck)
engagement.bounce_rate: % who leave after one page (< 50% is good)

tools.google_analytics: Page views, sessions, conversion funnels, user flow

types.click_heatmap: Where users click (find dead clicks, popular elements)
types.move_heatmap: Mouse movement (attention, though not perfect on mobile)
types.scroll_heatmap: How far users scroll (where to place CTAs)

rage_clicks.cause: Element not loading, broken link, confusing interaction

accessibility_testing.description: Ensure product is usable by people with disabilities

tools.lighthouse: Built into Chrome DevTools, accessibility audit

automated_testing.limitations: Catches ~30-40% of accessibility issues (needs manual testing)

color_contrast.requirement: WCAG AA: 4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text (18px+)

assistive_testing.description: Test with real users with disabilities
assistive_testing.recruit: Recruit users who use assistive tech daily

performance_testing.description: Test speed and technical performance (UX impact)

user_interviews.description: One-on-one conversations to understand needs, behaviors, pain points

question_techniques.five_whys: Ask 'why' to get to root cause
question_techniques.tell_me_about: 'Tell me about the last time you...' (concrete stories)
question_techniques.avoid_leading: Don't suggest answers ('Do you like X?' → 'How do you feel about X?')

best_practices: Probe on interesting points ('Tell me more')

contextual_inquiry.description: Observe users in their natural environment while they work

structure.master_apprentice: User is expert, you are learning
structure.observe: Watch user work on real tasks
structure.interrupt: Ask questions when you see something interesting

contextual_inquiry.output: Workflow diagrams, pain points, workarounds, environment factors

diary_studies.description: Users log activities over time (days to weeks)
diary_studies.when: Understand behavior over time, in context

personas.when: After user research, to communicate findings

journey_mapping.description: Visualize user's experience over time across touchpoints

structure.stages: Steps in the journey (Awareness → Consideration → Purchase → Use)
structure.touchpoints: Where user interacts (website, app, email, support)
structure.actions: What user does at each stage

best_practices: Test early and often (not just at the end)
Test with real users (not coworkers or friends)
Recruit representative users (screen for target audience)
Use think-aloud protocol in usability tests
Don't lead or guide users (let them struggle)
Combine quantitative (analytics, A/B tests) and qualitative (interviews, usability tests)
Share findings with team (video clips are powerful)
Test accessibility (keyboard, screen reader, color contrast)
Run A/B tests long enough for statistical significance
Test one variable at a time in A/B tests
Use standardized surveys (SUS, NPS) for benchmarking
Create personas based on research (not assumptions)
Test information architecture before visual design (tree testing)
anti_patterns: Testing with coworkers or friends (not representative)
Leading users ('Click here', 'Did you see...')
Stopping A/B test too early (before significance)
Testing too many variables at once
Skipping research phase (designing based on assumptions)
Not testing on mobile devices
Ignoring accessibility testing
Only doing quantitative OR qualitative (need both)
Creating personas without research
Not prioritizing findings (trying to fix everything)
Testing too late (after development complete)
Not recruiting representative users
Insufficient sample size (< 5 users per test)
Not testing across browsers
Confusing correlation with causation in analytics
Testing in unrealistic scenarios
Not validating findings with stakeholders
Focusing only on what users say (ignore what they do)
Not documenting research (insights lost)
Testing without clear goals or hypotheses
references: Don't Make Me Think (Steve Krug) - Usability fundamentals
Rocket Surgery Made Easy (Steve Krug) - DIY usability testing
The User Experience Team of One (Leah Buley) - Practical methods
Nielsen Norman Group - Research articles and courses
Just Enough Research (Erika Hall) - Practical research guide
Quantifying the User Experience (Sauro & Lewis) - Metrics and statistics
Measuring the User Experience (Tullis & Albert) - UX metrics
